paper_title: Characterizing High-Capacity Janus Aminobenzene–Graphene Anode for Sodium-Ion Batteries with Machine Learning

perturbations.why_wrong: The text discusses sodium storage and intercalation in anodes; sodium ions are cations (Na$^+$), not anions (Na$^-$).
The context refers to sodium ion accommodation in SIBs (Sodium-ion batteries), where sodium exists as a monovalent cation (Na$^+$), not a divalent cation.

The context describes electrostatic interactions between like-charged ions in a metal cluster/intercalation context; Na$^-$ is chemically inconsistent with the described Na$^+$--Na$^+$ repulsion in sodium-ion anodes.
Changing the charge of the sodium ion to 2+ contradicts the standard monovalent state of sodium in electrochemical energy storage systems mentioned throughout the paper.
The text lists electrostatic interactions; a neutral Na$^0$ would not participate in the electrostatic Na--Na repulsion or cation-pi interactions described in the same sentence.
The material is Janus graphene functionalized with aminobenzene/amine groups; NH$_2$ is the standard amino group, whereas NH$_3^-$ is chemically incorrect for this covalent attachment.

Cation-pi interactions are a specific type of non-covalent interaction involving the pi-system of aromatic rings (like graphene/benzene); cation-sigma interactions are not the standard mechanism for ion-adsorption on graphene.
An anode material for SIBs requires a low voltage to maximize the full-cell potential; 1.5 V is too high for a 'desirable' anode voltage compared to the 0.15 V typical for carbon-based intercalation.

The text describes a 'stable desirable voltage' of 0.15 V; using the much-less-than symbol suggests the voltage is significantly lower than the value provided, which contradicts the specific value given.

The text describes 'consistent high ion-diffusivity'; 10$^{-12}$ cm$^2$/s is several orders of magnitude lower than the 10$^{-6}$ cm$^2$/s range typically considered 'high' for fast-charging battery anodes.
